source.reference: SciBench dataset, source: Engel & Reid 'Thermodynamics, Statistical Thermodynamics, & Kinetics', problem 1.3. Verified by manual calculation: P = RT/(V_m - b) - a/V_m^2 = (0.083145 * 426)/(1.31 - 0.0320) - 1.355/(1.31^2) = 35.419/1.278 - 1.355/1.7161 = 27.72 - 0.79 = 26.93 bar.

problem.statement: Calculate the pressure exerted by Ar for a molar volume of 1.31 L/mol at 426 K using the van der Waals equation of state. The van der Waals parameters a and b for Ar are 1.355 bar dm^6 mol^-2 and 0.0320 dm^3 mol^-1, respectively. Determine whether the attractive or repulsive portion of the intermolecular potential is dominant under these conditions.
problem.task: Calculate: (1) the van der Waals pressure, (2) the ideal gas pressure for comparison, (3) the repulsive pressure contribution (RT/(V_m - b)), (4) the attractive pressure correction (a/V_m^2), and state which intermolecular force dominates.

molar_volume.description: Molar volume of Ar (note: 1 L = 1 dm^3)

vdw_param_a.description: van der Waals attraction parameter for Ar

vdw_param_b.description: van der Waals volume exclusion parameter for Ar

gas_constant_R.description: Gas constant in units compatible with the given parameters

vdw_pressure.description: Pressure from van der Waals equation: P = RT/(V_m - b) - a/V_m^2

ideal_gas_pressure.description: Ideal gas pressure for comparison: P_ideal = RT/V_m

attractive_term.description: Attractive correction: a/V_m^2 (subtracted from repulsive term)

vdw_pressure._comment: Closed-form calculation, tight tolerance. Rounding in intermediate steps may cause small variation.

must_include: Use the van der Waals equation of state
Identify the repulsive and attractive contributions separately
Compare to ideal gas behavior to determine which correction dominates
must_not_include: Use the ideal gas law as the final answer
Ignore unit consistency between R, a, b, and V_m

domain_context.recommended_thermo_model: van der Waals equation of state (simplest cubic EOS, appropriate for demonstrating non-ideal corrections)
key_assumptions: van der Waals equation is adequate for Ar under these conditions
Parameters a and b are temperature-independent constants
common_mistakes: Using the wrong gas constant units (R must be in dm^3 bar mol^-1 K^-1 to match a and b)
Forgetting that 1 L = 1 dm^3 (not converting molar volume)
Adding the attractive term instead of subtracting it
Not computing the ideal gas comparison, making the qualitative question unanswerable
Mixing up which term is repulsive vs attractive

reasoning_checkpoints.checkpoint: Selects the correct form of the van der Waals equation: P = RT/(V_m - b) - a/V_m^2
Ensures unit consistency — uses R in dm^3 bar mol^-1 K^-1 or converts appropriately
Calculates repulsive and attractive terms separately before combining
Computes ideal gas pressure as a reference point
Correctly identifies that attractive forces dominate because P_vdw < P_ideal
Notes that corrections are small because Ar is a noble gas with weak intermolecular forces

repulsive_greater_than_total._comment: The attractive correction always reduces pressure below the repulsive-only value

vdw_below_ideal._comment: At these conditions for Ar, attraction dominates so P_vdw < P_ideal
